Did they wake up that morning and say, you know what? You know, Josh, he owns that marketing agency. I need leads. Just tell me how much it's gonna cost, and I'll tell you if I'm interested Did the prospect plan that out before they got on the sales conversation with you? No! That's a triggered reaction. Somebody said lack of information. That doesn't cause objections. Lack of certainty causes objections. Information alone does not cause a prospect to have 100% certainty. Information is research mode. I've got lots of information. I'm researching everything, but that doesn't mean they're emotionally going to buy.
Information is logical. How do human beings make buying decisions? On emotion, not logic. Brain size, there's no debate. There is literally Zero debate on this in behavioral science. That's what I went to school for. There's no debate on how your brain makes decisions. Nobody's brain makes decisions logically. It all starts with your emotions. Let me show you. I feel like a drink of water. feel like a drink of water. Because I'm thirsty. That decision started with my emotional side of the brain. I feel like I feel thirsty. So I need a drink of water because I want to quench that thirst. Logical. I justify logic. I feel like doing this training call for you to help you sell more. I feel like you feel like listening to me because you want to learn how to sell more.
See your decisions, every decision you make starts with your emotional side of the brain. There's no decision you will ever make on your logical side of the brain. As a human being, you can't. If your emotional side of your brain gets damaged in a car accident, you're a vegetable. You can't decide to go pee. You can't decide to drink water. You can't decide to eat food. You can't decide to talk. Every decision you make starts with your emotional side of the brain. Then you justify it with logic. Okay? And it's how you actually ask the questions in the first place that causes the prospect to either emotionally open up and that builds certainty. Now, lack of uncertainty, lack of certainty is what causes objections. Now, what causes a prospect to have certainty in what you're offering?
There's many things. I could do a 50-hour training on that. I'll give you a little overview. Lack of certainty in a prospect is caused by the salesperson, not by here's a bunch of information that does not cost certainty. Certainty is caused by you. Lack of certainty or certainty. Now you can achieve 100% certainty or very close to that by what questions you ask. And how you ask them with the right tone that causes the prospect to internalize and think deeper about the question you asked. Some of you ask your questions so fast that you literally give your prospects no time to internalize what you asked, and that's why they give you vague, generalized, surface-level answers. Surface-level answers are logical-based, and they never open up emotionally, okay?

It's just easier. Okay? Now, never buy into the myth that the more objections a prospect gives you, You ever heard a sales trainer says, the more objections you get, the more interested they are.
How the hell does that make any sense? Anybody have any evidence that the more objections you get, the more interested they are? That's just stupid. That doesn't make any sense. Think about all the lay down sales you got. They didn't give you any objections. So how does that make sense? Take this to the bank. The more objections you get from your prospect, the more uncertainty you have triggered in their brain and less likely they are to buy. Take that to the bank every time. The less objections you get, because you get good at preventing them. You will sell way more. Doesn't mean you'll never get one, but it's way easier to help a prospect overcome one. Okay, if you've triggered more certainty in their brain, you have less objections and they will be way easier for you to help them overcome.

You've got two options when you see that.
Okay, so you have two options when you see that. The first option is, is you can do this. This is what most salespeople do right here. Option number one, this is probably what 99% of salespeople would do. They would hope and pray that everything's okay. And keep going through their pitch. And hopefully that wins Karen over and everybody else. That's what most salespeople do. They just hope and pray. That whatever Karen just did with her body language, okay, that it's going to be okay. They'll just keep going through their presentation and it's going to work. Hope and pray method. We call that hopium. That's a drug that most salespeople take. That is a very hard and unpredictable way to make a living as a salesperson or to really scale your business to where you could be if you run the business.
Very hard. Now, my second option is. Should we find out what is behind Karen giving you that nonverbal message? Through her body language, to prevent an objection when you try to close. Answer that, so I prevent that objection from happening after the presentation or proposal when I'm trying to close. Do you see what I'm doing there? You seem a bit hesitant. What's going on now? You don't want to get on a sales call and, in the first 30 seconds, if you like, trigger sales resistance. And 30 seconds later, you're like, 'Hey, you seem a bit hesitant.' What's going on? Click. You can't ask that question too quickly in a conversation because you don't have— hardly any trust or credibility—within the first 30 seconds if the prospect's a bit mean to you.
You can't then say, 'You seem a bit hesitant, John.' What's behind that? They're going to be like, 'F off.' and click, 'Okay?' That is not what I mean. That's further in the conversation if you can sense something's going on, okay? And there's more trust and credibility. That's when you'd use some of that type of stuff. Or I could say it like this. 'Hey, Sally, when I asked you about blank, you seemed like you... You didn't really want to talk about that. Can you tell me what's going on?' answer for you?' And the prospect says, 'Yeah, yeah, yeah, sure.' Now, what did I just hear? Most salespeople, when they get like, 'Like, yeah, yeah, sure,' they're like, 'Okay, great.
